content: The single biggest mistake aspiring founders make is deliberately trying to think of startup ideas. This process produces bad ideas — ones that sound plausible but lack genuine demand. The right approach is to stop looking for ideas and instead become the kind of person who notices problems organically, especially problems you personally experience.
implication: Don't run brainstorming sessions to generate your startup idea. Instead, build expertise at the frontier of a field, pay attention to friction you encounter daily, and trust that the best ideas will surface from lived experience rather than deliberate invention.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: The best startup ideas come from living at the frontier of a field — being among the first people to experience a world that hasn't arrived yet for most people. When you're ahead of the curve, you notice gaps and missing tools that others can't see because they haven't reached that point yet. The idea is simply what's missing from the future you already inhabit.
implication: Invest in being genuinely technically or culturally ahead. Read the newest research, use the newest tools, work on the hardest problems. The startup ideas will emerge as natural byproducts of operating at the frontier rather than as deliberate inventions.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Startup ideas that seem too small or too obvious are often the most valuable ones. Founders dismiss them precisely because they seem too easy or too niche, but that perception is the filter keeping competition away. The best ideas frequently look like toys or curiosities at first — they only reveal their true scale in retrospect.
implication: Don't discard an idea because it seems embarrassingly simple or small. Ask instead whether a real person would urgently want this, and whether you're in a unique position to build it. Apparent obviousness is a feature, not a bug.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Graham coined the concept of 'schlep blindness' — the unconscious filtering out of startup ideas that involve tedious, unglamorous, or painful work. Smart people systematically avoid imagining themselves doing difficult operational work, which means the ideas requiring it go unfunded and unbuilt. The willingness to do the schlep is itself a competitive advantage.
implication: Deliberately scan for ideas you've been unconsciously avoiding because they seem like a pain to execute. If a problem is genuinely important and the work to solve it is genuinely unpleasant, that's a signal — not a deterrent. Your competitors have filtered it out too.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Graham's core test for a startup idea is whether a small number of people want it urgently, not whether a large number of people want it mildly. A tiny group of users who desperately need something is a far stronger foundation than a broad audience with vague interest. Most failed startups made something nobody wanted badly enough — not something nobody had heard of.
implication: Optimize your early product for intensity of need, not breadth of appeal. Find the ten users who cannot live without what you're building, and build for them obsessively before worrying about reaching the next thousand. Desperate need creates the feedback loop that builds great products.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: The ideal startup idea sits at the intersection of three conditions: something you're able to build, something you're genuinely interested in, and something that few others are currently working on. The third condition is the hardest to satisfy and the most underweighted by founders, who often build things in crowded spaces without realizing it.
implication: Before committing to an idea, rigorously audit the competitive landscape — not just direct competitors but also the adjacent solutions people are already using. A technically feasible idea in a space nobody is working on is worth far more than an elegant idea in a crowded market.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Graham distinguishes between ideas that seem good and ideas that are good. Ideas that merely 'seem good' — big markets, obvious problems, clean pitches — are dangerous because they attract too much competition too quickly and often lack the authentic demand that drives real growth. Ideas that are genuinely good often seem weird, risky, or too small at first.
implication: Be suspicious of startup ideas that are easy to pitch and immediately make sense to investors. The best ideas require explanation, come with caveats, or seem to have obvious problems. That friction is often what's kept competitors away and kept the opportunity alive.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: One of the most reliable sources of startup ideas is noticing when something that used to be impossible has recently become possible. Technology shifts — cheaper compute, new APIs, regulatory changes, new platforms — open windows that didn't exist before. Founders who are paying close attention can step through those windows before anyone else realizes they're open.
implication: Track technology inflection points carefully. When a new capability or platform emerges, immediately ask what it makes newly possible. The best time to build on a new substrate is early, before the opportunity becomes obvious and crowded.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Graham argues that working on problems you personally experience is not just convenient — it's strategically superior. Founders who are solving their own problems have a built-in advantage: they are the customer, they feel the pain acutely, and they can evaluate their own solutions with direct experience rather than secondhand research. Trying to imagine other people's problems is far weaker.
implication: Before surveying potential customers or conducting market research, ask yourself honestly: do I personally have this problem? If yes, trust that signal heavily. If you're solving a problem you've never experienced, invest in becoming a genuine user before building anything.
source_ref: Lecture 3 - Before the Startup (Paul Graham), Stanford / How to Start a Startup 2014

content: Most startup ideas that get funded are solutions looking for problems rather than problems looking for solutions. Founders pick a technology or mechanism they find interesting and then rationalize a market around it. This gets the causality backwards. The right sequence is to start with a real, urgent problem and only then figure out what to build.
implication: Audit whether your startup began with a problem or a solution. If you started with the solution, go back and rigorously verify that the problem is real and urgent to actual people — not just plausible to investors. Many startups that fail do so because the problem was invented, not discovered.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: The organic path to startup ideas is to get good at something, work on hard problems, and pay close attention to what frustrates you. The idea generation is a side effect of deep engagement with a domain — not the output of a separate ideation process. This is why the best founders typically have genuine expertise rather than generalist opportunism.
implication: The fastest path to a great startup idea may be to spend a year becoming genuinely expert at something difficult and paying obsessive attention to what's broken. Domain expertise combined with frustration tolerance is a more reliable engine for opportunity discovery than any structured ideation method.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Ideas that seem obvious to the founder but aren't obvious to others are often the most valuable. Graham calls this the 'obvious to us, unknown to them' asymmetry. It usually exists because the founder has lived through an experience or works in a domain that most investors and observers haven't — which makes the idea look risky from the outside when it's actually de-risked by insider knowledge.
implication: Don't abandon an idea just because outsiders don't immediately get it. If you understand why the idea is good in a way that requires real experience to appreciate, that asymmetry is valuable. The goal is to be right, not to be immediately understood by a general audience.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Graham frequently points to Airbnb as an example of an idea that looked obviously bad to most observers but turned out to be one of the best startups of its era. The idea of strangers sleeping in each other's homes seemed either too niche or too weird. This shows that the conventional 'smell test' for startup ideas systematically filters out good ones alongside bad ones.
implication: Be cautious about using social proof and conventional plausibility as your primary filter for ideas. If your idea passes the smell test easily, ask whether it's differentiated enough. The ideas that seem clearly good to everyone are often already being built by someone with more resources.
source_ref: Lecture 3 - Before the Startup (Paul Graham), Stanford / How to Start a Startup 2014

content: Graham notes that startup ideas exist on a spectrum from 'things that seem good and are' to 'things that seem bad but are good.' The most dangerous quadrant is things that seem good but aren't — they waste years of founder time and investor capital. The most underexplored quadrant is things that seem bad but are actually good — and that's where outlier returns come from.
implication: Develop a practice of examining the ideas in your space that smart people have dismissed. Ask whether the dismissal was based on genuine domain knowledge or on pattern-matching to something superficially similar. A defensible reason why the conventional wisdom is wrong is the core of a great investment thesis — or a great startup.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Graham describes a class of opportunities he calls 'new things that are underrated because they're new.' When a new technology or behavior first appears, it is systematically underestimated because most people evaluate it using frameworks built for what came before. The founders who correctly assess the new thing early — not after it's been validated — are the ones who build category-defining companies.
implication: Treat emerging technologies and behaviors as objects of serious study, not curiosities to revisit later when they're more mature. The right time to engage deeply is when the technology still looks like a toy. By the time it looks like an opportunity, the early advantage is gone.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Graham observes that good startup ideas often start in a market that seems too small to bother with. The key insight is that the best markets don't look big at first — they start small and grow as the product creates or expands the market. Targeting a small, intensely engaged early market and expanding from there is a more reliable path than trying to address a large market from day one.
implication: Resist the pressure — especially from investors — to demonstrate a large total addressable market before you've earned it. Start with the smallest viable market where you can achieve genuine intensity of use, then use that beachhead to expand. The story of 'this is small now but will be huge' is more credible when you can show the small part working.
source_ref: Paul Graham on why starting with a 'small, intense fire' is the key to startup growth

content: One reliable method for finding startup ideas is to look at things that have recently changed — in technology, regulation, culture, or infrastructure — and ask what those changes make newly possible. Change creates temporary windows where the established players haven't adapted and the new entrant has a structural advantage. Startups are built for windows, not equilibria.
implication: Build a regular practice of identifying changes in your environment — new platforms, new regulations, new consumer behaviors — and immediately asking what they unlock. The startup opportunity lives in the gap between the change and the market's adaptation to it, and that gap closes faster than founders expect.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Graham argues that the best way to prepare yourself to find startup ideas is to go deep on something real — build things, solve hard problems, get into the guts of difficult domains. The surface-level observer who spends time reading about startups and thinking about markets will always be outcompeted by the person who has actually built something and lived with its constraints.
implication: Prioritize doing real technical or operational work over consuming startup media or attending networking events. The intellectual terrain for good ideas is built through doing, not through observing. The person who has built three projects, even failed ones, has a dramatically richer idea landscape than someone who has thought carefully but built nothing.
source_ref: Lecture 3 - Before the Startup (Paul Graham), Stanford / How to Start a Startup 2014

content: Graham points out that many of the best startup ideas were once considered 'recipes for failure' by experienced observers. What looks like naivety in a founder is sometimes a genuine insight that conventional wisdom has calcified around incorrectly. The history of successful startups is substantially a history of founders ignoring consensus and being right.
implication: Cultivate the ability to hold a position that contradicts experienced opinion and rigorously test whether the conventional view is actually grounded in evidence or just in incumbency. The fact that smart people have dismissed your idea is evidence worth examining — but it is not, by itself, dispositive.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: According to Graham, the most dangerous version of the question 'is this a good idea?' is when it's answered by committee or consensus. Ideas that survive social evaluation tend to be the ones that are legible, safe, and already validated — which are exactly the properties that make an idea less likely to be a great startup. Good ideas often fail social validation tests before they succeed in the market.
implication: Be extremely cautious about running your startup idea through informal networks for approval before stress-testing it against real users. Advisors and peers optimize for plausibility; customers optimize for need. These are different filters and often produce opposite verdicts.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Graham's framework treats the startup idea as an artifact of the founder's experience and position, not as a free-floating object to be discovered through research. The question isn't 'what ideas are out there?' but 'given who I am, what I know, and what I've experienced, what problem am I uniquely positioned to see and solve?' This reframes idea generation as identity and positioning work.
implication: Invest in understanding your own unique vantage point before searching for ideas. What industries have you worked in? What tools have frustrated you? What do you understand that most people don't? Your competitive advantage as a founder starts with the specificity of your position, not the quality of your brainstorming.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com

content: Graham is skeptical of startup ideas pitched as 'X for Y' where X is a successful company and Y is a new demographic or geography. These ideas are legible and easy to pitch but suffer from a fundamental problem: the original X usually succeeded for reasons that are deeply context-specific, and those reasons often don't transfer. Derivative ideas rarely become great companies.
implication: If your startup idea can be described as 'Uber for X' or 'Airbnb for Y,' push yourself to articulate what is genuinely new about your insight beyond the format. The analogy may help investors understand your market, but it should not be the actual intellectual foundation of the company.
source_ref: Lecture 3 - Before the Startup (Paul Graham), Stanford / How to Start a Startup 2014

content: Graham has noted that the ideas that become the biggest companies often require the founder to believe something specific about the future that most people don't believe yet. This is not vague optimism — it's a precise, falsifiable belief about how the world is changing. The founder's job in the early stage is to be right about that belief before the market validates it.
implication: Articulate the specific belief about the future that your startup depends on. Make it precise and falsifiable. If you can't state what you believe that others don't, and why you're likely to be right, you don't yet have the intellectual core of a great startup — regardless of how well the product is built.
source_ref: How to Get Startup Ideas — paulgraham.com
